pr_title: Allow for two day names shorter than 3 letters (#343)
pr_body:
Possibly a fix for issue #343 

Issue summary: Short day names had to be at least 3 characters long to be recognized, which works for the default day names (`Mon`, `Tue`, ...) but not necessarily for custom `WEEKDAYS` names such as German day name abbreviations (`Mo`, `Di`, ...).

* This PR changes the check from a comparison against a constant value (`3`) to a value calculated from the entries of the `WEEKDAYS` setting. 
* Since the same applies to `MONTHS`, applying the equivalent change there. 
* Added a test for the case of German day names, which fails before the change and passes after.

A possible next step would be to cache the return value of `min(len(n) for n in self._weekdays.keys())` to avoid re-computation. Please let me know if that would be preferred here.

Added optional directory display and image grouping to the image browser
pr_body:
While implementing the module into my site I figured it would be handy if the image browser displayed directories, as well as grouped the images so they don't show up as a big wall of thumbnails with no way of figuring out when they were uploaded and/or used. So I've developed this small feature - if enabled, the image browser now displays directories and thumbnails of images that belong to them, sorted by date.

Enabling the grouping requires setting the ``CKEDITOR_BROWSE_SHOW_DIRS`` to ``True``. My code iterates through the ``files`` list of dictionaries right before passing it to the template, and creates a set of unique directories out of images present in ``files``, then sorts it using the built-in ``sorted()`` function. 

``dir_list = sorted(set(os.path.dirname(f['src']) for f in files), reverse=True)``

Context contains two additional elements: ``show_dirs``, a boolean taken from the ``CKEDITOR_BROWSE_SHOW_DIRS`` setting, as well as ``dirs``, a sorted list of directories.

The ``browse.html`` template changes are self-explanatory. For every directory, display the directory path and iterate through files checking if the path matches, which essentially results in images getting grouped by directory they're in. Commit 866c242 shows a previous implementation that I've decided to change. It took less lines, however with a downside: code executed even if the feature was disabled, which basically means you couldn't truly disable the feature. I've decided to put the ``{% if show_dirs %}`` statement first and duplicate the contents of ``<ul class="thumbs noscript">`` - more duplicated lines and sort of less readable at first glance, but easier to understand what is going on, and actually works as intended.

There is an additional fix in the view, for removing Thumbs.db files from the list, an issue I ran across while testing. I guess it could stay, given it's enclosed in conditional statement and will only run on Windows. If there's a better place for that, please let me know.

I've also updated readme to take note of the new feature.

pr_title: Fix inaccessible python-dateutil deserialization logic by adding a configuration parameter to DateTime
pr_body:
The existing implementation of `fields.DateTime` contains an [inaccessible code path](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/fields.py#L959-L967) that appears to have intended fall back to `python-dateutil` for deserialization in cases when a `dateformat` is not defined.  It even contains a warning message suggesting that `python-dateutil` be installed, but the logging of this message is also inaccessible.

The code is inaccessible because it only runs if `self.dateformat` is `None`, but `self.dateformat` is always re-assigned to `self.DEFAULT_FORMAT` in an [earlier line](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/fields.py#L947).

The changes I made here enable format-free deserialization via `python-dateutil`, requiring the user to "opt-in" by setting a parameter when constructing the `DateTime` field, e.g.:
```python
class MySchema(Schema):
    dt = DateTime(auto_deserialize_format=True)
```
I considered implementing this as a fallback, rather than an opt-in parameter, as the existing code seemed to be written.  However, it is clear that this could have unintended consequences: for example, one of the unit tests requires that a year-only input date be rejected, while `dateutil` will parse a year-only input using *today's date* as the source of default values for all missing fields, which is quite unintuitive (IMO).

Also, as another minor change, the reassignment of `self.dateformat` described above is both unnecessary and unexpected, as a typical user would probably not assume that `deserialize()` or `serialize()` methods would mutate class variables.  While it seems unlikely that this would ever have caused a problem, it still should probably be avoided, so I have changed both `serialize()` and `deserialize()` to eliminate this pattern.

I added a unit test to confirm that the code does correctly parse non-standard date formats when so configured (and not otherwise).  All other existing unit tests continue to pass.

pr_title: Included field might not be set
pr_body:
I have three models:
 * Account
 * AccountTypeA
 * AccountTypeB

AccountTypeA/AccountTypeB each have a OneToOneField to Account. Account can only be of type A or B, not both. In my AccountSerializer I've included the attributes for both the reversed relationships. I've also included the AccountTypeA/AccountTypeB serializers in included_serializers.

extract_included expects both fields to be present on every account instance when in reality each account will have either the A or B relation. This pull request makes extract_included to ignore missing fields.

This is a rework of #459 that is no longer a "half-baked" (@nunoplopes) solution as it can now build the necessary generated files itself and so can function with needing to invoke from the Python build system first.

The last commit lists some of the many advantages this build system has.

It will need some additional work to work under MSVC and to support building/installing/uninstalling the python, java, dotnet and ml language bindings but this can be done in the future.

To give this a try do

```
# You need a clean source tree (i.e. no generated files in the source tree) otherwise CMake will
# complain. First check you are happy with what will be deleted (``-n`` is a dry run).
git clean -nx
# Now delete the untracked files for real
git clean -fx
# Make a build directory
mkdir build
cd build
cmake -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=/whatever/install/prefix/you/want   /path/z3/repository/
make -j8
make c_example
make cpp_example
make test-z3 -j8
# Try installing
make install
# Try uninstalling
make uninstall
```

So far I've tested with make and ninja on Arch Linux with CMake 3.4.3. I will be testing more platforms shortly.

This PR addresses several problems I've been having with the credentials code from an LHCb perspective.

 1. Added `DiracProxy` tests
 2. Added the ability for a `DiracProxy` to _not_ add the default group extension to a proxy filename as it may or may not be on disk already. This fixes the problems for lhcb users as they will likely already have or need to use a 'normal' proxy with other tools. I've discovered that you can create/destroy proxies using something like: `credential_manager.create(DiracProxy())` and `credentialManager.destroy(DiracProxy())`.

I'll add a further PR shortly with some improved documentation around this and I think it may be worth adding a wrapper for something like `gridProxy.renew()` for LHCb users to smooth the transition to 6.3.

pr_title: Test the RPM rysnc distrib's fast-forward logic
pr_body:
Expand `PublishTestCase` in module
`pulp_smash.tests.rpm.api_v2.test_rsync_distributor`. The test now
performs the following steps, where steps 4 and 5 are new:

1. Create a repository with a yum distributor and RPM rsync distributor.
   Add content units to the repository.
2. Publish with the yum distributor.
3. Publish with the RPM rsync distributor. Verify that the correct files
   are in the target directory.
4. Remove all files from the target directory. Publish again, and verify
   that:

   * The task for publishing has a result of "skipped."
   * No files are placed in the target directory. (This tests Pulp's
     fast-forward logic.)

5. Publish with the RPM rsync distributor, with `force_full` set to
   true. Verify that files are placed in the target directory. Skip this
   step if Pulp issue 2202 [1] is not yet fixed.

As part of this expansion, refactor out some test logic into methods.

[1] https://pulp.plan.io/issues/2202

> Django template loaders have been updated to allow recursive template

> load_template() and load_template_sources() methods are now

origin since the proper template can no longer be disambiguated by

pr_title: Fix dtype bug on systems where longdouble is equivalent to double
pr_body:
In testing the recently merged #189 on a Windows system using the conda-forge FFTW libraries, I came across 2 test errors.  The conda-forge libs have single and double precision only.

It turns out the errors were both related to the fact that numpy on that system has a `longdouble` dtype that is identical to `double`.  For example, both of the following evaluate to true:
```python
np.dtype(np.longdouble) == np.dtype(np.float64)
np.dtype(np.clongdouble) == np.dtype(np.complex128)
```

This leads to a subtle bug when we set `_all_types_np` in `pyfftw.pyx`.  This currently uses
_all_types_np = {
    np.dtype(np.float32): '32',
    np.dtype(np.float64): '64',
    np.dtype(np.longdouble): 'ld',
}
However, on the system I tested on the last 2 keys are equivalent so `_all_types_np` only ends up with 2 entries:  ` {dtype('float32'): '32', dtype('float64'): 'ld'}`

There is a similar issue where tests are expecting a `NotImplementedError` to be raised for `np.clongdouble dtype`, but since this is actually equal to `np.complex128` it does not fail as expected. 

For reference I paste the exact test failures that this fixes here:
```
================================== FAILURES ===================================
_______________________ FFTWPartialTest.test_conversion _______________________

self = <test.test_pyfftw_partial.FFTWPartialTest testMethod=test_conversion>

    def test_conversion(self):
        self.conversion('float32', 'float64', 'longdouble')
>       self.conversion('float64', 'longdouble', 'single')

test\test_pyfftw_partial.py:102:
_ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _
test\test_pyfftw_partial.py:89: in conversion
    self.assertEqual(b.dtype, _rc_dtype_pairs[alt2.char])
E   AssertionError: dtype('complex128') != dtype('complex64')
________________________ FFTWPartialTest.test_failure _________________________

self = <test.test_pyfftw_partial.FFTWPartialTest testMethod=test_failure>

    def test_failure(self):
        for dtype, npdtype in zip(['32', '64', 'ld'], [np.complex64, np.complex128, np.clongdouble]):
            if dtype not in _supported_types:
                a = empty_aligned((1,1024), npdtype, n=16)
                b = empty_aligned(a.shape, dtype=a.dtype, n=16)
                msg = "Rebuild pyFFTW with support for %s precision!" % _all_types_human_readable[dtype]
                with self.assertRaisesRegex(NotImplementedError, msg):
>                   FFTW(a,b)
E                   AssertionError: NotImplementedError not raised

test\test_pyfftw_partial.py:64: AssertionError
```

pr_title: Version 2 of ACMEv2 support for Route53 plugin
pr_body:
Based on #5661 and an alternative to it.

When testing #5661 after carefully following the instructions in the package and from Jacob, I realized that this adds the need for permission `route53:ListResourceRecordSets`. While not at all something I thought about before, I think this is a problem. Suddenly changing the required permissions could break automated renewal for existing users.

We took over this plugin from https://github.com/lifeonmarspt/certbot-route53 who built it for use in their organization and we went through a lot of effort (including making the `authenticator` shim you find in the package and the dummy package on PyPI) not to break anything. Additionally, our DNS plugins in general have been released for many months now, so there are people who are comfortable with Python and/or persistent who are currently using the plugins.

This PR fixes the problem without any change in behavior. That is:

* We don't need any new permissions.
* If you had existing TXT records at the validation domain, they are blown away.
* Attempting to delete a record another value was set elsewhere is an error.

While #5661 is more technically correct, preserving existing records at `_acme-challenge` domains is unlikely to matter and I think not adding new permissions is worth it.

pr_title: Silence Package Manager Output when certbot-auto invoked with --quiet
pr_body:
Add the following flags when `certbot-auto --quiet` is invoked:
- Add `-qq` to calls to `apt-get` in Debian
- Add `--quiet` to calls to `yum` or `dnf` in CentOS or Fedora
- Add `--quiet`to calls to `urpmi` in Mageia
- Add `--quiet` to calls to `pkg install` in FreeBSD

Changes were made to `letsencrypt-auto-source/pieces/bootstrappers/*` and were seen reflected in `letsencrypt-auto-source/letsencrypt-auto` after running `letsencrypt-auto-source/build.py`, but did not appear to propagate to `certbot-auto` or `letsencrypt-auto`.  I suppose this relates to #3703; if I can get a better understanding of how auto-updating of these scripts works, I can work on #3703 too.  

Also of note:  running `letsencrypt-auto-source/build.py` propagated changes from 8c1aa3ef to `letsencrypt-auto-source/pieces/letsencrypt-auto`.  I noticed in #3410 that @bmw requested that `build.py` be run but it does not seem like the script was run (or that the changes made by the script were committed).  My fork and branch are based off of the current `HEAD` of `certbot/certbot`, so I'm assuming that this omission was simply overlooked when merging #3410 in.  The changes from running `build.py` were made in a separate commit (e1ca6dc) to facilitate easier issue tracking; I can cherry-pick this commit and open a separate PR for that issue if doing so is preferred.  

---

In terms of design decisions for the code in this PR, it is possible (and likely) that the script execute such that `$QUIET_FLAG` is uninitialized.  This may be problematic if `set -u` is set, as the script will exit with an error.  

On a more esoteric level, uninitialized variables can be an issue for destructive commands; to use a canonical example, if the following function is invoked without an argument, we're probably not going to have a nice day:
```
function remove_bin() {
    # I have no idea why this function would exist in the first place...
    DIRECTORY=$1
    rm -rf $DIRECTORY/bin
}
```

But since he `$QUIET_FLAG` evaluating to an empty string leaves the package manager commands syntactically and functionally valid (and in fact equivalent to the commands currently being run), I opted to follow the convention set forth by `$YES_FLAG` and left `$QUIET_FLAG` uninitialized.  If the script exiting if `set -u` is set is deemed problematic, I'd be more than happy to initialize `$QUIET_FLAG` and `$YES_FLAG` in a separate Issue/PR.  

(Resolves #3515)

If you attempt to do `plots_from_files` passing a list containing 9 filepaths with rows = 2, you will get the following error:
```ValueError: num must be 1 <= num <= 8, not 9```

This minor change prevents the error from occurring. In the above scenario, 2 rows of images would be plotted, with 5 and 4 images per row.

My intent was to create a data class that could replace the trick that allows us to use ImageClassifierData for detection.

Things were going relatively okay until I encountered transforms. I have taken only a cursory glance at how they work however I am not sure what would be a nice way of getting them to work with this.

Also, I am not really sure this is the architectural direction we want to take. Might be I have dabbled in something that doesn't make sense for me to look into at this point. Might be this effort is completely unneeded and the only feasible way of getting a class for data for detection would be via a bigger refactoring that would include transforms.

Happy for this to be closed, only thought I'd share this in case there was value in working on this further. Don't suspect that this is the case but if it is then happy to continue with the effort but would require guidance how to proceed.

pr_title: add dispersion to the small_delay value
Improve delays to sleep only during needed time
pr_body:
**Context:**
We want to separate likes from each other by `like_delay`;
Currently the bot sleeps for this delay always, even if the elapsed time since the last like is already greater than the delay;
**This pull requests ensures that the bot will sleep only if needed.**

**Why it's good ?**
Because we can now mix and match actions and wait less overall while still ensuring the bot won't be banned by Instagram.
For instance, with a like_delay=10 and follow_delay=30
We can do the sequence of actions: `follow; like; like; like; follow` in ~30 seconds instead of ~60 seconds.

**Only 31 lines**

What I purpose with this PR:

- Code simplification, it is little messed in IDE when you see all methods twice
- PEP8 cleanup
- Switch to mixin type of inheritance

I hope it will help to maintain and develop code in future.
Pretty routine stuff, but I think this gets trio._util to 100% coverage.

pr_title: [identities] Load identities to sortinghat by bulks
pr_body:
This PR changes the way of loading identities to sortinghat. In a nutshell, instead of loading in memory all the identities before sending them to sortinghat, they are sent to bulks of 500. Thus, the consuption of memory is safe when dealing with a large number of identities. Furthermore, this PR enables also to log exceptions that may occur when processing identities. This log can help to shed some light on mordred
threads that from time to time die without leaving any trace.

Refs: https://dev.plone.org/plone/ticket/12276

The set of changes in proposed merge from my fork: (a) navigation.pt now renders a non-site navigation root navtree node with a content icon using root_icon() method already defined in navigation.py (this likely predates Plone 4, and is previously unused in Plone 4, but seems convenient and correct); (b) CHANGES.txt updated accordingly.

For normal plone site roots, the standard site icon sprite is used.  The site icon makes much less sense in other navigation root contexts, though.

Rationale: sites using explicit navigation root strategies -- where a content item (folder) provides INavigationRoot for a lightweight sub-site like setup -- will, more often than not, want to keep the content icon for that item as root node icon in the navigation portlet [1].  The current use of a plone site icon sprite (via CSS) is only reasonable for a site.  This change provides a more reasonable default for icon behavior than the status quo, and does not exclude integrators wanting to keep the old behavior of a site icon from registering an IContentIcon multi-adapter for that very purpose with just a few lines of ZCML (normal icon, not a sprite).  

I posted to plone-developers seeking comments from anyone using sub-sites about this change.

Sean

[1] By way of example, my "sub-site" (groupware workspace acting as a nav root) uses a "home folder" icon, which is less visually ambiguous than using the site icon, especially for users who traverse more than one navigation root in the site (we have a link to the site root in a viewlet for this purpose).
